baseline_categories.description: Dense-528 and successors on the same local hardware, token budget, dataset, and evaluation harness.
Best model that runs comfortably on one workstation, quantized when useful, with quality plus tokens/sec, VRAM, RAM, and size measured.
API or separately served GLM-5.2 used as external frontier reference, target, teacher, and oracle; never treated as same-budget unless run under identical local constraints.
Official or third-party GLM-5.2 benchmark numbers, marked as not reproduced unless the same harness is run in this repository.

contenders.current_status: candidate stack definition; functional tokenizer and packed quantization evidence still missing
contenders.name: Conservative local coder
contenders.next_ablation: Dense-528 byte tokenizer versus D5 BPE on executable JavaScript and API-reuse tasks, then add structured memory.

contenders.current_status: partial proxy evidence; fast weights need a second real chronology target and security gates
contenders.name: Modular repository learner
contenders.next_ablation: Structured memory alone versus structured memory plus fast updates on executable or API-reuse chronology.

contenders.current_status: not ready for model training; graph extraction and Hebbian feature ablations must improve first
contenders.name: Novel mechanism contender
contenders.next_ablation: Typed-edge graph extraction and repository-local Hebbian features must beat lexical and no-Hebbian trained ranker baselines.

glm_5_2_track_policy.local_deployment_policy: Estimate download size, disk, RAM, VRAM, runtime, engine support, ROCm compatibility, and multi-GPU/NVIDIA requirements before downloading large GLM-5.2 weights; require explicit approval before hundreds of GB downloads.
glm_5_2_track_policy.private_data_policy: Never send confidential company code, private prompts, private repository content, secrets, credentials, customer data, or restricted documents to any remote GLM-5.2 service.
glm_5_2_track_policy.published_reference_policy: Published GLM-5.2 numbers are external references until the same benchmark, split, scoring method, tool access, generation budget, and harness are reproduced here.
success_criteria: Published coding benchmark numbers recorded from primary sources.
Reproducible GLM-5.2 evaluation harness for public tasks.
At least one local model compared against GLM-5.2 on the same public tasks.
Teacher data tested against a no-teacher control.
At least three GLM-inspired local-scale experiments recorded.
No private data sent to a remote model.
Final report separates same-budget local results from external frontier reference results.
glm_5_2_track_policy.teacher_data_policy: All GLM-5.2 teacher samples must be labeled synthetic and trace source task, prompt, model, settings, generation date, validation result, test status, and filter/reject status.

current_evidence: T12 selects dense-528 by mean final/best validation loss, throughput, model bytes, VRAM, and gradient stability.
T11c executable JavaScript syntax probe records syntax-valid completions but is syntax-only.
ideas.intended_benefit: Strongest current local validation/resource/throughput baseline for later coding experiments.
known_incompatibilities: Does not by itself provide repository memory or continual adaptation.
Free-form QuixBugs repair generation currently fails.
ideas.mechanism: Small dense causal decoder trained on D4 code data with AdamW FP32 and finite causal transformer blocks.
ideas.name: Dense-528 AdamW FP32 local decoder
required_assumptions: Validation loss remains predictive enough to choose architecture before public benchmark gates.
D4/D5 functional tasks expose coding utility beyond loss.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Run D4/D5 executable JavaScript and QuixBugs-style repair gates against T11c and a matched alternative.
strongest_opposing_evidence: T11b residual-adapter keeps a reported-only final-test-loss edge and best causal-span token accuracy.
T11c free-form QuixBugs greedy and sampled repair candidates repair 0/4 tasks.

current_evidence: T11b had a small final-test-loss edge and strong causal-span token accuracy.
Identity initialization stabilized an earlier adapter candidate.
ideas.intended_benefit: Possible quality/resource frontier alternative to dense baselines.
known_incompatibilities: Current implementation is not parameter-efficient in the usual frozen-base sense.
T12 validation selection favors dense-528.
ideas.mechanism: Adapter-style residual modules inside a small decoder with all parameters trainable in current experiments.
ideas.name: Residual-adapter-528 decoder
required_assumptions: Adapter path gives task quality advantages that survive validation-focused selection.
Future frozen-base or partial-training variants can recover usual adapter deployment benefits.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Run matched functional coding probes against dense-528 with identical prompts and held-out tasks.
strongest_opposing_evidence: T12 selects dense-528 on validation/resource/throughput criteria.
Earlier adapter debug runs preserved nonfinite-gradient failures before identity initialization.

current_evidence: D5 BPE reduces train tokens by about 3.08x and validation tokens by about 2.92x.
Equal-compute pilots improve exact nats/raw byte over byte-token pilots.
ideas.intended_benefit: Improve context coverage and train-byte throughput versus byte tokens.
known_incompatibilities: Equal-raw-byte BPE pilot was worse than byte tokens in current loss accounting.
Functional quality remains unmeasured.
ideas.mechanism: Train an 8192-vocab byte-level BPE tokenizer over sampled D5 code documents to reduce sequence length.
ideas.name: D5 trained byte-level BPE tokenizer
required_assumptions: Token reduction improves functional coding quality or cost at equal raw-byte exposure.
Tokenizer artifacts remain reproducible and license-safe.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Evaluate byte-vs-BPE checkpoints on executable JavaScript syntax/API-reuse/repair tasks with equal raw-byte exposure.
strongest_opposing_evidence: BPE equal-raw-byte run is worse than byte tokens.
No executable functional tokenizer comparison has been recorded yet.

current_evidence: E6 structured-memory series passes symbol, signature, call-stub, skeleton, docstring, API reference, coverage, and drift proxies.
MarkupSafe API documentation coverage QA answered 4/4 with structured docs memory while source-symbol-only answered 0/4.
D5 repository-context pairwise validation scores structured symbol memory at hit@5 0.8696 with proxy hallucinated API rate 0.0 on 23 tasks, still beating the best retrieval variant.
ideas.intended_benefit: Improve repository understanding, documentation lookup, and safety without retraining weights.
known_incompatibilities: Not code generation by itself.
Requires extraction/index maintenance and trust labels.
ideas.mechanism: Store symbols, signatures, documentation directives, paths, and trusted metadata in explicit queryable structures.
ideas.name: Structured external repository memory
required_assumptions: Structured extraction remains accurate enough on real repositories.
Explicit memory can combine with dense models without causing stale or unauthorized answers.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Pair structured memory with dense generation on repository-local API reuse and measure hallucinated API rate.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Some tasks remain exact lookup proxies rather than full code generation.
Extraction quality for broad JavaScript package graphs is still weak.

current_evidence: Synthetic IF2 fixture shows fast weights answer 7/8 paraphrase queries where retrieval and structured memory answer 0.
IF4 MarkupSafe fast weights plus retrieval match structured/replay top-5 future changed-file accuracy.
ideas.intended_benefit: Continual repository adaptation without permanent unsafe drift.
known_incompatibilities: Fast weights alone underperformed on MarkupSafe changed-file prediction.
Security and poisoning gates are incomplete for real model updates.
ideas.mechanism: Scoped reversible fast-weight updates from verified chronological local examples with rollback accounting.
ideas.name: Signed fast-weight evolution scratchpad
required_assumptions: Fast updates add value beyond retrieval and structured memory on real repository tasks.
Rollback and poisoning gates remain cheap enough for local use.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Run a second real repository chronology target with executable/API-reuse scoring and rollback-cost accounting.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Fast weights alone underperform updated retrieval and structured memory on real MarkupSafe chronology.
Synthetic IF2 lacks poisoning/security gates.

current_evidence: IF7 sparse assembly beats frequency and random controls on real D5 masked recall.
IF7i task-aware ranker beats static lexical/raw Hebbian baselines.
ideas.intended_benefit: Repository-local associative recall and candidate generation from sparse memory.
known_incompatibilities: Dense full-score Hebbian conditioning loses to cue-only after larger D5 window runs.
Current trained repository ranker loses to its no-Hebbian ablation.
ideas.mechanism: Sparse co-activation assemblies over repository/text patterns, optionally used for conditioning, reranking, or repository linking.
ideas.name: Sparse Hebbian repository memory
required_assumptions: Hebbian features must add value beyond cue-only, lexical, and no-Hebbian trained baselines.
Sparse features can be made repository-local or typed-edge enough to help decoder tasks.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add typed-edge or repository-local Hebbian features and require a win over the no-Hebbian trained ranker.
strongest_opposing_evidence: IF7b/c/d/h cue-only trained models beat cue-plus-Hebbian validation.
IF7i no-Hebbian trained ablation beats the Hebbian-feature ranker.

current_evidence: D5 repository API-reuse validation builds 23 tasks and symbol-name mention reaches hit@5 0.8696.
ideas.intended_benefit: Reduce hallucinated API rate and improve practical coding correctness.
known_incompatibilities: Current D5 validation proxy is symbol selection, not executable generation.
Train-to-validation split produced no tasks under current repository split.
ideas.mechanism: Score whether a system selects existing repository-local functions/symbols instead of hallucinating new APIs.
ideas.name: Repository-local API reuse objective
required_assumptions: Symbol extraction labels are clean enough for training/evaluation.
Selection gains transfer to generated code.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Expand labels and require model or memory-assisted method to beat symbol-name mention on held-out repositories.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Small validation-only task set across 3 repositories.
No executable runtime scoring yet.

current_evidence: BF16-all and uniform int8 stay close to FP32 for T11 final checkpoints on D4 quantization samples.
ideas.intended_benefit: Practical local deployment with smaller model size and limited quality loss.
known_incompatibilities: Current probes reconstruct into FP32 PyTorch tensors and do not benchmark packed kernels.
Uniform int4 degrades sharply.
ideas.mechanism: Post-training uniform int8 quantization of trained checkpoints with measured loss and storage.
ideas.name: Uniform int8 checkpoint quantization
required_assumptions: Loss-preserving quantization remains close on functional tasks.
Packed runtime kernels exist or can be implemented on ROCm.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Benchmark packed int8/BF16 runtime on loss-preserving policies with executable functional probes.
strongest_opposing_evidence: No packed int8/int4/BF16 runtime measurement yet.
Protected int4 carrier policies are not robust.

current_evidence: Learned reconstruction beats random reconstruction control on MSE and validation loss.
ideas.intended_benefit: More aggressive compression than simple quantization while preserving behavior.
known_incompatibilities: Current reconstruction degrades validation loss badly.
FP32 runtime reconstruction erases deployment value.
ideas.mechanism: Learn block codebooks plus residual accounting for trained checkpoint compression.
ideas.name: Audited block-codebook weight generator
required_assumptions: Codebook reconstruction can preserve validation loss at useful compression.
Runtime accounting can avoid FP32 reconstruction buffers.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Try less destructive low-rank plus groupwise residual compression and require held-out loss near FP32 without FP32 runtime buffers.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Validation loss degrades from FP32 T11c 1.0335 to learned reconstruction 5.3102.
Encoded plus runtime bytes exceed practical deployment target.

current_evidence: Repairs 4/4 selected QuixBugs Python tasks with 6 deterministic candidates.
ideas.intended_benefit: Calibrate repair harness and provide a target for model ranking/generation.
known_incompatibilities: Not model-generated and not scalable as a product repair method.
ideas.mechanism: Hand-engineered syntax-preserving AST edits over buggy QuixBugs Python programs.
ideas.name: Deterministic QuixBugs AST-edit baseline
required_assumptions: Small selected tasks remain useful as a smoke test before larger repair benchmarks.
Candidate provenance is clearly labelled as non-model.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Expand to additional QuixBugs tasks and measure candidate count explosion and repair rate.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Hand-engineered for the selected smoke lane.
Does not show language-model coding ability.

current_evidence: T11c ranks 4/4 passing deterministic edits first in the six-candidate pool.
Four-program bounded top-k syntax-pool execution repairs 4/4 at top-8.
After the counter-zero mutation, the 12-program slice repairs 4/12 by top-8 under dense likelihood, tied with repair-aware and seeded random on program rate.
ideas.intended_benefit: Use model signal for repair selection even when free-form generation fails.
known_incompatibilities: Free-form dense generation still fails.
Likelihood ranking over too broad a pool may select plausible but wrong edits.
ideas.mechanism: Use trained dense checkpoint teacher-forced likelihood to rank syntax-valid repair candidates before pytest evaluation.
ideas.name: Dense checkpoint repair-candidate likelihood ranking
required_assumptions: Model likelihood ranking correlates with repair correctness on broader candidate pools.
Candidate generation can become less hand-constrained while preserving syntax.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add learned or failure-localized ranking and require it to beat repair-aware and multiple random controls on the 12-program slice after candidate coverage improves beyond 4/12.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Free-form dense generation still repairs 0/4 selected QuixBugs tasks.
Four-program controls beat dense on candidate budget.
The 12-program counter-zero run does not show dense likelihood beating repair-aware or random same-pool controls on program repair rate.

current_evidence: Four-program smoke builds 63 syntax-valid candidates and contains passing candidates for all four tasks.
The first 12-program slice builds 189 syntax-valid candidates and contains passing selected candidates for 3/12 programs.
Adding final-counter-zero mutation builds 190 candidates and improves selected passing coverage to 4/12 programs.
ideas.intended_benefit: Move repair candidates away from the narrow deterministic baseline while avoiding invalid Python.
known_incompatibilities: Candidate pool can include many plausible wrong edits.
Top-1 T11c ranking currently fails most selected tasks.
ideas.mechanism: Generate syntactically valid AST alternatives using generic binary operator, comparison, return constant, call-argument, yield, guard, and residual mutations.
ideas.name: Broader syntax-preserving AST mutation pool
required_assumptions: Generic syntax mutations contain enough useful repairs without too many plausible distractors.
Model ranking can choose passing candidates from the wider pool.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add another non-oracle mutation family and require the 12-program QuixBugs gate to move beyond 4/12 passing coverage without worsening same-pool controls.
strongest_opposing_evidence: The pool is still hand-designed AST mutation scaffolding and only repairs a narrow subset of Python QuixBugs tasks.
After the counter-zero mutation, 8/12 programs still have no passing selected candidate, so candidate generation coverage remains the main bottleneck.

current_evidence: On the four-task smoke, repair-aware static ordering repairs 4/4 programs at top-1 and beats deterministic order, dense likelihood, and seeded random order.
On the 12-program counter-zero run, repair-aware ordering repairs 3/12 at top-1 and 4/12 by top-8, showing useful early placement for the new counter-zero candidate.
ideas.intended_benefit: Lower executable validation budget by placing likely repair candidates before broad syntax distractors.
known_incompatibilities: Hand-engineered pattern ordering is not model capability evidence.
Narrow repair patterns can overfit benchmark families or miss multi-file bugs.
Weak tests can reward patching to the harness rather than true repair.
ideas.mechanism: Rank syntax-preserving repair candidates with non-oracle AST repair signals such as empty guards before star-unpack, value/type isinstance shape, recursive modulo progress, and squared residual checks.
ideas.name: Repair-aware static syntax-pool ordering
required_assumptions: Static repair-pattern signals generalize beyond the four selected QuixBugs tasks.
The selector can be expanded from hand-coded patterns to failure-localized or learned repair-aware ordering without oracle source access.
Candidate provenance and execution results remain recorded for every promoted run.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add failure-localized or learned repair-aware features and require top-1/top-4 gains over deterministic, dense-likelihood, and multiple random controls on the 12-program slice.
strongest_opposing_evidence: The 12-program result still falsifies the four-task top-1 selector claim as a general result.
Dense likelihood, repair-aware ordering, and seeded random tie at 4/12 by top-8 after the counter-zero mutation, so static repair-aware ordering is not a robust selector win.

current_evidence: IF1 graph signal probe found typed edges and preserved repository-aware splits.
ideas.intended_benefit: Improve repository understanding, API reuse, and documentation consistency.
known_incompatibilities: Current D5 graph edges are sparse and partly heuristic.
No model pilot should run before extraction improves.
ideas.mechanism: Condition generation on typed repository graph edges such as import-local, doc-to-source, and test-to-source links.
ideas.name: Repository graph conditioned decoder
required_assumptions: Graph extraction becomes non-heuristic enough to support training.
Graph features add value beyond structured memory and lexical baselines.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Implement AST/package-aware JavaScript import resolution and require material edge growth without docstring/source self-links.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Only 12 locally resolved import edges in the first improved extractor.
No trained graph-conditioned model evidence yet.

current_evidence: Primary RAG work supports combining parametric and non-parametric memory for generation.
RepoBench frames repository-level retrieval and completion as separable measurable tasks.
Project E6 structured-memory proxies already show repository-local external context can answer documentation/API questions.
D5 repository-context pairwise validation is now recorded as a reduced proxy comparison against structured symbol memory.
Symbol-aware retrieved snippets reduce proxy hallucinated API rate to 0.0, improving over raw retrieved identifiers.
Query-symbol-aware retrieval improves hit@5 to 0.7826 with proxy hallucinated API rate 0.0.
ideas.intended_benefit: Improve repository understanding and reduce hallucinated API use without storing all repository knowledge in model weights.
known_incompatibilities: Can become redundant with structured_repository_memory if retrieval returns only exact symbol facts.
Stale indexes can inject obsolete APIs unless rebuild provenance is tracked.
ideas.mechanism: Retrieve repository-local files, symbols, or snippets and condition generation or ranking on that non-parametric context.
ideas.name: Retrieval-augmented repository context

primary_sources.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
RepoBench: Benchmarking Repository-Level Code Auto-Completion Systems

required_assumptions: Retrieved context is relevant enough to improve local coding tasks.
The retrieval index can be built explicitly without silent network calls or private-data leakage.
Context-window and latency costs remain practical for local inference.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add structured-plus-retrieval context or repository-path-aware tie-breaking and require a gain over structured symbol memory on held-out API reuse before dense prompt integration.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Project tasks have not yet shown dense generation improves when retrieval context is appended.
Exact lookup proxies may overstate utility for executable code generation.
Raw retrieved-snippet identifier extraction scores hit@5 0.0 and proxy hallucinated API rate 0.9826 on the D5 validation API-reuse proxy.
Symbol-aware retrieved snippets score hit@5 only 0.2174, far below structured symbol memory at 0.8696.
Query-symbol-aware retrieval is a near miss but still below structured symbol memory: hit@5 0.7826 versus 0.8696.

current_evidence: Primary LoRA paper reports strong downstream adaptation with many fewer trainable parameters on larger language models.
Project residual_adapter_528 result shows adapter-like paths can train, but did not prove the frozen-base LoRA mechanism.
ideas.intended_benefit: Support modular local specialization with smaller update files, rollback, and lower training cost than full fine-tuning.
known_incompatibilities: Current residual_adapter_528 experiments were fully trainable and are not evidence for frozen-base LoRA benefits.
Small base models may lack enough pretrained capability for low-rank adaptation to dominate full fine-tuning.
ideas.mechanism: Freeze a base model and train low-rank update matrices for repository or task adaptation.
ideas.name: Low-rank repository adaptation

primary_sources.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

required_assumptions: A frozen or mostly frozen base checkpoint is strong enough to benefit from low-rank updates.
Repository-specific low-rank updates can be evaluated and rolled back independently.
The local model implementation can support LoRA modules without breaking ROCm compatibility.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Train a frozen dense-528 low-rank repository adapter on D5 API-reuse labels and require held-out improvement over prompt-only structured memory plus full fine-tune control accounting.
strongest_opposing_evidence: T12 currently selects dense-528 over the residual-adapter lane.
No Dark Nova frozen-base low-rank run has shown functional coding gains.

current_evidence: Self-Refine and Reflexion primary papers support test-time iterative improvement from model or environment feedback.
Project QuixBugs harness already has executable pass/fail feedback for selected repair tasks.
Bounded pytest execution over repair-aware syntax-pool candidates can find 4/4 repairs at top-1 on the four-task smoke.
ideas.intended_benefit: Turn executable failures into repair signals without immediately retraining model weights.
known_incompatibilities: Unbounded loops conflict with reproducible local resource limits.
Weak test suites can reward overfitting or patch-minimality failures.
ideas.mechanism: Use bounded test, compiler, or verifier feedback to revise generated patches or ranked candidates at inference time.
ideas.name: Execution-feedback self-refinement loop

primary_sources.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning

required_assumptions: Feedback is local, deterministic, and cheap enough to run under project resource limits.
The loop improves real executable success rather than only optimizing to weak or leaky tests.
Each iteration records seed, command, failure status, and candidate provenance.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Run a bounded two-iteration feedback loop only if it can beat repair-aware static ordering on a larger subset or reduce candidate generation assumptions.
strongest_opposing_evidence: The current positive result is static repair-aware ordering plus validation, not an iterative model-generated revision loop.
A feedback loop must beat a strong top-1 static repair-aware baseline, not just dense top-k ranking.

current_evidence: Agentless primary work reports strong open-source SWE-bench Lite performance from a simpler localization/repair/validation pipeline.
SWE-agent primary work supports explicit agent-computer interfaces for software engineering tasks.
Project QuixBugs candidate lanes already separate candidate generation, ranking, and pytest validation.
ideas.intended_benefit: Make local coding repair more interpretable, cheaper to ablate, and less dependent on a large autonomous agent stack.
known_incompatibilities: Full SWE-agent-style autonomous tool use may exceed first-pass local resource and reproducibility constraints.
Pipeline quality is bottlenecked by the weakest stage.
ideas.mechanism: Separate repository issue solving into explicit localization, patch generation, and validation stages rather than a single free-form completion.
ideas.name: Agentless localization-repair-validation pipeline

primary_sources.title: Agentless: Demystifying LLM-based Software Engineering Agents
Agent-Computer Interfaces Enable Automated Software Engineering

required_assumptions: Local models can perform at least one stage well enough when paired with retrieval, structured memory, or candidate generation.
Stage boundaries expose failure causes instead of hiding them in a monolithic agent trace.
Validation can run locally with deterministic resource caps.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Build a local mini pipeline on QuixBugs or a tiny repository issue set and require a stage-level gain over direct dense completion plus deterministic AST baseline controls.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Current local dense free-form repair generation repairs 0/4 selected QuixBugs tasks.
Project has not yet implemented multi-file issue localization.

current_evidence: RepoBench primary paper defines retrieval, completion, and pipeline tasks for repository-level code completion.
Project D5 API-reuse and E6 structured-memory probes already target repository-local information, but not this benchmark.
ideas.intended_benefit: Measure repository understanding separately from single-file loss and syntax-only probes.
known_incompatibilities: Repository completion is not the same as executable patch repair.
Public dataset use must respect the no-raw-dataset-in-Git rule.
ideas.mechanism: Use repository-level retrieval, cross-file completion, and end-to-end pipeline tasks as a measurement gate.
ideas.name: RepoBench-style repository completion gate

primary_sources.title: RepoBench: Benchmarking Repository-Level Code Auto-Completion Systems

required_assumptions: A reduced local slice can be created without exceeding dataset and disk limits.
Metrics can be reproduced without silent network downloads.
The gate correlates with practical repository assistance.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Create a documented reduced RepoBench-compatible local slice and require at least one contender to beat lexical retrieval/completion baselines before using it as a promotion gate.
strongest_opposing_evidence: No RepoBench slice has been downloaded, pinned, or evaluated in this repository.
Completion accuracy alone may miss test-passing patch behavior.

current_evidence: SWE-bench Verified is a 500-instance human-filtered subset of SWE-bench.
It remains useful as historical context and for comparison with prior work.
ideas.intended_benefit: Compare contender stacks on real repository issue resolution tasks.
known_incompatibilities: Full SWE-bench-style runs are likely too expensive for first-pass local experiments.
Primary-source evidence now warns that SWE-bench Verified is contaminated and no longer a frontier measure.
ideas.mechanism: Use the human-filtered SWE-bench Verified issue set as the main external benchmark for scaling decisions.
ideas.name: SWE-bench Verified as primary scale-up gate

primary_sources.title: Why SWE-bench Verified no longer measures frontier coding capabilities

required_assumptions: The benchmark remains representative of practical coding capability.
The local project can run the benchmark within resource limits.
Contamination and test-quality risks are low enough for scale-up decisions.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Do not promote it as the primary scale-up gate unless a small pinned local slice proves affordable and a newer uncontaminated benchmark is unavailable.
strongest_opposing_evidence: OpenAI's 2026 primary-source analysis says SWE-bench Verified is increasingly contaminated and recommends SWE-bench Pro instead for frontier evaluation.
It is not aligned with the current small, CPU-compatible first-pass constraint.

current_evidence: Z.ai reports GLM-5.2 uses IndexShare and claims 2.9x lower per-token FLOPs at 1M context for the indexer path.
GLM-5.2 reports strong long-horizon coding benchmark scores, including Terminal-Bench 2.1, SWE-bench Pro, FrontierSWE, PostTrainBench, and SWE-Marathon.
ideas.intended_benefit: Make million-token repository and agent-trajectory contexts cheaper without relying only on retrieval truncation.
known_incompatibilities: Current Dark Nova dense decoder uses full finite-causal attention and does not implement DSA-style sparse attention.
A 744B-A40B GLM-scale design is outside current single-local-GPU training constraints.
ideas.mechanism: Reuse one sparse-attention indexer across groups of transformer layers so top-k sparse attention indices are computed less often at long context.
ideas.name: IndexShare sparse attention for long-context coding

primary_sources.title: GLM-5: from Vibe Coding to Agentic Engineering

required_assumptions: The local architecture uses or can be changed to a sparse attention mechanism with reusable routing indices.
Long-context coding quality is limited by attention/indexing cost rather than only by data, model size, or KV cache capacity.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Prototype a tiny shared-index sparse-attention block or retrieval-plus-shared-index surrogate and require better long-context repository completion cost/quality than structured memory plus dense baseline on a pinned local slice.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Evidence is external vendor/model-card reporting, not a Dark Nova reproduction.
Z.ai notes KV-cache capacity and long-context kernel overhead remain bottlenecks even with reduced per-token computation.
Current local experiments have not shown that million-token context is the limiting factor versus repository memory or executable repair quality.

current_evidence: The MTP rejection-sampling paper reports up to 25 percent extra inference throughput and up to 1.8x async RL-training acceleration on Qwen-family models.
Z.ai reports GLM-5.2 improves MTP acceptance length by up to 20 percent for coding scenarios using IndexShare/KVShare, rejection sampling, and TV loss.
ideas.intended_benefit: Increase inference and RL-rollout throughput for local coding agents without changing the primary model quality target.
known_incompatibilities: Current Dark Nova checkpoints do not include an MTP head or speculative decoding runtime.
The first useful local target may be inference speed, not RL training acceleration, because Dark Nova does not yet run RL post-training.
ideas.mechanism: Train a multi-token prediction draft path with rejection sampling and end-to-end total-variation loss to raise accepted speculative tokens during code and agentic generation.
ideas.name: MTP rejection-sampling speculative decoding

primary_sources.title: Breaking Entropy Bounds: Accelerating RL Training via MTP with Rejection Sampling

required_assumptions: The model has enough capacity and training budget to support an auxiliary MTP path.
Throughput, not only quality, is a limiting factor for local iterative repair and long-horizon agent loops.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Add a tiny auxiliary next-token or two-token draft path to a small local checkpoint and require measured wall-clock generation speedup on executable repair prompts with unchanged pass rate.
strongest_opposing_evidence: External results are not reproduced in Dark Nova and use larger model/training infrastructure.
Speculative decoding improves runtime only if candidate acceptance is high enough and implementation overhead does not dominate on ROCm.

current_evidence: Primary Z.ai/Hugging Face sources report GLM-5.2 long-context and coding benchmark results, including Terminal-Bench 2.1, SWE-bench Pro, FrontierSWE, PostTrainBench, SWE-Marathon, NL2Repo, DeepSWE, and ProgramBench.

Z.ai developer docs expose API-facing capabilities relevant to local comparison: 1M context, 128K max output, thinking mode, function calling, context caching, structured output, and project-level engineering use cases.

GLM5_2_public_eval_harness creates an offline public/synthetic/approved-data harness with privacy gates, saved-output scoring, required prompt/context/output/cost/latency/token metadata checks, and a three-task synthetic smoke pack.
T11c_dense528_glm_public_smoke compares the current same-budget local dense checkpoint on the harness and scores 0/3 under greedy ROCm decoding with complete metadata.
Dark Nova currently has no reproduced GLM-5.2 scored output, no GLM-5.2 teacher-data experiment, and no local GLM-5.2 deployment feasibility record.
ideas.intended_benefit: Quantify the gap between Dark Nova local models and a frontier open-weight coding model, and test whether GLM-5.2 outputs or mechanisms improve local practical coding ability.
known_incompatibilities: GLM-5.2 is far outside the current same-budget local training setup and cannot be used as evidence that Dark Nova has frontier capability.
Remote API evaluation is incompatible with private code, credentials, customer data, or restricted documents.
Large local GLM-5.2 downloads require explicit approval after storage/RAM/VRAM/runtime feasibility estimates.
ideas.mechanism: Use GLM-5.2 as a separated external benchmark reference, black-box public-task evaluator, synthetic teacher, and architecture inspiration source while preserving local same-budget baselines.
ideas.name: GLM-5.2 external baseline and teacher track
required_assumptions: GLM-5.2 access is available through published reports, API, or approved local serving without exposing private data.
Public evaluation tasks are sufficient to compare behavior without leaking private repositories.
Teacher-generated data can be filtered by executable tests, static checks, or source-grounded validation instead of blind imitation.
ideas.smallest_falsifying_test: Score saved GLM-5.2 outputs on the public harness under explicit prompt, budget, tool, cost, latency, token, and judge metadata; then test one GLM-5.2 teacher-data slice against a no-teacher control.
strongest_opposing_evidence: Published numbers are external and may use different harnesses, tool access, generation budgets, and scoring methods than Dark Nova.
A 744B-A40B class model is not a same-budget local baseline for the current workstation path.
Teacher data can improve style imitation without improving held-out executable coding ability unless controlled against no-teacher baselines.

pairwise_test_queue.test: Repository API-reuse prompt augmentation with hallucinated API rate.
Executable JavaScript syntax/API-reuse comparison at equal raw-byte exposure.

pairwise_test_queue.result: QuixBugs_T11c_dense528_counter_zero_syntax_controls_12prog: final-counter-zero mutation increases the 12-program selected repair coverage from 3/12 to 4/12; dense likelihood reaches 4/12 by top-8, tied with repair-aware static order and seeded random on program rate.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Keep expanding non-oracle syntax-pool candidate generation, then retest dense ranking against repair-aware and random same-pool controls.

pairwise_test_queue.result: Counter-zero mutation gives repair-aware order 3/12 repairs at top-1 and 4/12 by top-8, an early-placement improvement but not a robust selector win because dense/random also reach 4/12 by top-8.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Add failure-localized repair-aware features and require top-1/top-4 gains on the 12-program slice.
Second repository chronology target with rollback and executable/API-reuse scoring.
AST/package-aware graph extraction followed by API-reuse scoring.

pairwise_test_queue.result: D5_repository_context_pairwise_validation: structured_symbol_memory hit@5 0.8696 and proxy hallucinated_api_rate 0.0; raw retrieved_snippet_identifiers hit@5 0.0 and proxy hallucinated_api_rate 0.9826; symbol_aware_retrieved_snippets hit@5 0.2174 and proxy hallucinated_api_rate 0.0; query_symbol_aware_retrieval hit@5 0.7826 and proxy hallucinated_api_rate 0.0. Retrieval remains revise because the best retrieval variant is a near miss but does not beat structured memory.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Compare retrieved snippets versus structured memory facts on repository API-reuse and hallucinated API rate.

pairwise_test_queue.result: Candidate coverage moved from 3/12 to 4/12 with a generic mutation. A feedback loop should improve uncovered failure modes before ranking the same weak pool harder.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Bounded feedback loop or failure-localized generator versus deterministic, repair-aware, dense, and random controls on the 12-program slice.
Repository adaptation with rollback accounting: frozen low-rank update versus scoped fast-weight update.
Local mini Agentless pipeline with retrieval-assisted localization and executable validation.

pairwise_test_queue.result: New external GLM-5.2 source triaged only; no local implementation yet.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Compare a tiny shared-index sparse-attention or long-context surrogate against structured repository memory on a pinned repository completion slice.

pairwise_test_queue.result: New external MTP/rejection-sampling source triaged only; no local implementation yet.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Measure local speculative/draft generation speedup on repair-loop prompts without reducing executable pass rate.

pairwise_test_queue.result: GLM5_2_public_eval_harness records an offline public/synthetic/approved-data harness and three-task smoke pack. T11c_dense528_glm_public_smoke scores the local same-budget dense checkpoint at 0/3 with complete metadata. No saved GLM-5.2 outputs have been scored yet.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Score saved GLM-5.2 outputs through the harness, then compare directly against the 0/3 T11c local baseline on the same tasks.

pairwise_test_queue.result: Goal addition recorded; no GLM-5.2 teacher-data experiment yet.
pairwise_test_queue.test: Generate public API-reuse or repair teacher samples, filter by tests/source-grounding, and train/evaluate against a no-teacher control.

purpose: Living portfolio for Dark Nova local coding-model ideas, combinations, contender stacks, and staged ablation planning.

stages.policy: Reject or revise ideas that fail their intended mechanism before scaling; promote pairwise combinations only when same-pool controls show measurable value at bounded scale and larger-slice follow-ups preserve the gain.
